quotes.verbatim_quote:
Practice playing your guitar with
records, listen to solos by horn players, learn to steal solos from records.
Anything that you hear another musician play, try to play it yourself. Strum
the chords to any song that you like and hum ideas, — then apply the ideas
to the guitar.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Codifies Baker's prescribed ear-training routine — records, horn solos, transcription by imitation, and hum-then-play — as the non-negotiable companion practice to the written exercises.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Another very important thing for you to do is to hang around jam sessions.
Do as much sitting with other musicians as possible. Doing this will help to
develop an ear for music and it also helps with your timing.
quotes.why_load_bearing: States that timing is developed socially in live ensemble play, not in solitary practice, framing the bandstand as essential pedagogy rather than optional.

quotes.verbatim_quote: Also, give each Lesson at least two hours study a day!
quotes.why_load_bearing: Sets the concrete daily-practice quantum Baker assumes for Part Two, calibrating reader expectations about the pace of progress through the solo material.

quotes.topic: 28 exercises as key to solo work
quotes.verbatim_quote:
First, we have exercises from Lesson 24 to 30. Each one is as important to you as eating and sleeping, because
within these 28 exercises lies the key to solo work. So you are to live with them for the rest of your life.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Identifies the specific span of exercises (Lessons 24-30, 28 exercises) as the lifelong technical foundation for everything that follows, marking them as the book's mechanical core.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Take each exercise and practice it as slowly
as possible. Only increase the tempo when you can play it relaxed at a slow tempo. Remember, if you cannot piay
an exercise slowly, you surely can’t play it fast.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Establishes the relaxed-slow-before-fast rule that governs how every exercise in Part Two is to be approached, a load-bearing technique-acquisition principle.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Every exercise and solo in this book is to be played with an up and down stroke. In order to play with an up

and down stroke you must start right now. Anything that you play from now on, you are to use up and down
strokes.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Imposes alternate picking as the universal right-hand requirement for the rest of the book, the picking-discipline rule all subsequent runs presume.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
You are to learn the name of these exercises by heart from one end of the guitar to the other. This will not only
make you more familiar with your instrument, but each of these runs will play a very important part in your solo
work,
quotes.why_load_bearing: Defines a naming/labeling discipline so the exercises function as a callable vocabulary later — you must be able to retrieve any run by name across the whole neck.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
The sign "X" is for open string.
The sign "S" is for the string.

The sign "F" is for the fingering.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Defines the symbol legend Baker uses across every exercise diagram in Part Two; without it the S./F. annotations under each exercise are unreadable.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
*Practice all exercises with Down and Up Strokes first, AVAVAYV.
Then with up and down. VAVAVAY.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Specifies the two-pass picking drill (down-up first, then up-down) that operationalizes the alternate-stroke rule and ensures the wrist is trained in both stroke orientations.
